posts.text: The pressure to produce numbers has changed how the system works: by November 2025, for every one person released from ICE detention pending a hearing, 14.3 were deported directly from custody compared to a roughly one-to-two ratio the year before.
posts.quote: The pressure to produce numbers has changed how the system works: by November 2025, for every one person released from ICE detention pending a hearing, 14.3 were deported directly from custody compared to a roughly one-to-two ratio the year before.

posts.text: Interior deportations rose 4.6 times between the second half of 2024 and September/October 2025. But even at that accelerated pace, the administration is on track for roughly 300,000 removals unprecedented, but far short of the million-per-year target they've been claiming.
posts.quote: Interior deportations rose 4.6 times between the second half of 2024 and September/October 2025. But even at that accelerated pace, the administration is on track for roughly 300,000 removals unprecedented, but far short of the million-per-year target they've been claiming.

posts.text: At-large arrests agents sweeping communities rather than executing targeted warrants increased by 600%. This is how you end up detaining a 19-year-old University of Utah student on her way to work and a U.S. citizen walking to lunch.
posts.quote: At-large arrests agents sweeping communities rather than executing targeted warrants increased by 600%. This is how you end up detaining a 19-year-old University of Utah student on her way to work and a U.S. citizen walking to lunch.

posts.text: Despite claiming to target the "worst of the worst," the data shows the opposite. ICE increased arrests of people with no criminal record by 2,450% in Trump's first year the most dramatic shift in enforcement profile in the agency's history.
posts.quote: Despite claiming to target the "worst of the worst," the data shows the opposite. ICE increased arrests of people with no criminal record by 2,450% in Trump's first year the most dramatic shift in enforcement profile in the agency's history.

posts.text: The Trump administration promised to deport one million people a year. One year in, the data tells a very different story about who is actually being deported, how it's happening, and what it's costing communities that never imagined they'd be targeted.
posts.quote: The Trump administration promised to deport one million people a year. One year in, the data tells a very different story about who is actually being deported, how it's happening, and what it's costing communities that never imagined they'd be targeted.

posts.text: The administration promised a stronger economy. Mass deportation delivers the opposite: higher deficits, slower growth, and higher prices. For expert analysis of the real costs of enforcement, follow: austinkocher.substack.com
posts.quote: The administration promised a stronger economy. Mass deportation delivers the opposite: higher deficits, slower growth, and higher prices. For expert analysis of the real costs of enforcement, follow: austinkocher.substack.com

posts.text: The agriculture sector employs undocumented workers in more than 1/3 of its positions. Construction, food service, healthcare are similarly dependent. Removing workers doesn't make American workers richer.
posts.quote: The agriculture sector employs undocumented workers in more than 1/3 of its positions. Construction, food service, healthcare are similarly dependent. Removing workers doesn't make American workers richer.

posts.text: Brookings estimates that net migration in 2025 was likely negative for the first time in at least half a century, which is already dampening labor force growth and consumer spending. Monthly job growth could turn negative in 2026 as a result.
posts.quote: Brookings estimates that net migration in 2025 was likely negative for the first time in at least half a century, which is already dampening labor force growth and consumer spending. Monthly job growth could turn negative in 2026 as a result.

posts.text: The Penn Wharton Budget Model estimates that a sustained mass deportation policy would cost an additional $900 billion over 10 years, even after the $170 billion already allocated in the One Big Beautiful Bill. Primary deficits would increase by $270 billion and GDP would shrink.
posts.quote: The Penn Wharton Budget Model estimates that a sustained mass deportation policy would cost an additional $900 billion over 10 years, even after the $170 billion already allocated in the One Big Beautiful Bill. Primary deficits would increase by $270 billion and GDP would shrink.

posts.text: Undocumented immigrants pay nearly $580 billion a year in taxes and hold $1.6 trillion in annual spending power. Mass deportation removes that from the economy while adding enormous enforcement costs at least $88 billion per year just to deport one million people annually.
posts.quote: Undocumented immigrants pay nearly $580 billion a year in taxes and hold $1.6 trillion in annual spending power. Mass deportation removes that from the economy while adding enormous enforcement costs at least $88 billion per year just to deport one million people annually.

posts.text: The Trump administration is billing mass deportation as a public safety measure. But economists, budget analysts, and researchers across the political spectrum have run the numbers and the costs are staggering. Here's what the data shows.
posts.quote: The Trump administration is billing mass deportation as a public safety measure. But economists, budget analysts, and researchers across the political spectrum have run the numbers and the costs are staggering. Here's what the data shows.

posts.text: An agency that violates court orders and detains U.S. citizens is not enforcing the law, it's flouting it. For clear, documented analysis of what the law actually says and how ICE is violating it, read: austinkocher.substack.com
posts.quote: An agency that violates court orders and detains U.S. citizens is not enforcing the law, it's flouting it. For clear, documented analysis of what the law actually says and how ICE is violating it, read: austinkocher.substack.com

posts.text: Early 2026, a fed judge in Minnesota identified 96 separate court orders ICE had violated across 74 cases since January 1st alone. At a Denver hearing, attorneys admitted that ICE arrest warrants are "riddled with mistakes" & agents couldn't explain what the court orders even say
posts.quote: Early 2026, a fed judge in Minnesota identified 96 separate court orders ICE had violated across 74 cases since January 1st alone. At a Denver hearing, attorneys admitted that ICE arrest warrants are "riddled with mistakes" & agents couldn't explain what the court orders even say

posts.text: In Minnesota, the ACLU sued after a 20-year-old U.S. citizen named Mubashir Hussen was stopped by masked ICE agents while walking to lunch. He repeatedly told them he was a citizen. They shackled him, took his fingerprints, and only released him after he showed his passport.
posts.quote: In Minnesota, the ACLU sued after a 20-year-old U.S. citizen named Mubashir Hussen was stopped by masked ICE agents while walking to lunch. He repeatedly told them he was a citizen. They shackled him, took his fingerprints, and only released him after he showed his passport.

posts.text: ICE kept making those arrests anyway. By February 2026, the ACLU filed a 98-page amended complaint alleging agents were still targeting people based on the color of their skin. Attorneys say ICE "has continued to thumb their nose both at the law and court orders."
posts.quote: ICE kept making those arrests anyway. By February 2026, the ACLU filed a 98-page amended complaint alleging agents were still targeting people based on the color of their skin. Attorneys say ICE "has continued to thumb their nose both at the law and court orders."

posts.text: In November 2025, a federal judge in Colorado ordered ICE to stop making warrantless arrests unless agents first establish probable cause that a person is both present illegally and a flight risk. The ruling called such arrests "unlawful."
posts.quote: In November 2025, a federal judge in Colorado ordered ICE to stop making warrantless arrests unless agents first establish probable cause that a person is both present illegally and a flight risk. The ruling called such arrests "unlawful."

posts.text: ICE is not just aggressive in case after case, courts are finding that the agency is operating outside the law. Federal judges across the country are issuing injunctions, and ICE is violating those orders too. Here's the pattern.
posts.quote: ICE is not just aggressive in case after case, courts are finding that the agency is operating outside the law. Federal judges across the country are issuing injunctions, and ICE is violating those orders too. Here's the pattern.

posts.text: A system without oversight is a system without accountability. For rigorous, data-driven analysis of immigration detention, including facility-by-facility numbers follow me at austinkocher.substack.com
posts.quote: A system without oversight is a system without accountability. For rigorous, data-driven analysis of immigration detention, including facility-by-facility numbers follow me at austinkocher.substack.com

posts.text: Oversight has collapsed alongside the expansion. Internal watchdogs have been cut, congressional inspection rights restricted, and ICE's detainee locator system has become so unreliable that people are effectively "disappearing" for days after arrest.

posts.text: A Closer Look at ICE's Enormous People Warehouse Near Hagerstown, Maryland. Watch now | I drove out to the site of one of the most controversial new ICE facilities in the country this afternoon, an 825,000 square foot warehouse built with the hopes of being used or sold to a logistics company like Amazon but which was recently purchased by Immigration and Customs and Enforcement for a million dollars to hold people instead of boxes.
posts.quote: A Closer Look at ICE's Enormous People Warehouse Near Hagerstown, Maryland. posts.text: Third ICE Custody Death This Week, 11th Since January 1. Pejman Karshenas Najafabadi, a 59-year-old Iranian national, was pronounced dead on Sunday, March 1, 2026 at Merit Health Hospital in Natchez, Mississippi, becoming the eleventh person to die in ICE custody since the start of the 2026 calendar year, at a rate that now averages one detained death approximately every six days.
posts.quote: Third ICE Custody Death This Week, 11th Since January 1. posts.text: Haitian Asylum Seeker Dies of Toothache in What ICE Calls "The Best Healthcare of Their Lives". Detention deaths are a foreseeable outcome of specific policy choices made by people in position of power. This post steps back to think through the broader trends and steps toward accountability.
posts.quote: Haitian Asylum Seeker Dies of Toothache in What ICE Calls "The Best Healthcare of Their Lives". Detention deaths are a foreseeable outcome of specific policy choices made by people in position of power. This post steps back to think through the broader trends and steps toward accountability.

posts.text: The Human Cost of Mass Detention: ICE Reports 9th Custodial Death of 2026, 38th of Trump II Administration. ICE press release for the death of Alberto Gutierrez-Reyes debuts a strikingly callous (and ungrammatical) new claim. Full table of all 38 deaths under Trump included in post.

posts.text: The Alternatives to Mass Immigrant Detention No One is Talking about w/ Claire Trickler-McNulty. Claire Trickler-McNulty and I discuss the non-detained docket, alternatives to mass detention, and why we're teaming up to research what actually works in the immigration system.
posts.quote: The Alternatives to Mass Immigrant Detention No One is Talking about w/ Claire Trickler-McNulty. Claire Trickler-McNulty and I discuss the non-detained docket, alternatives to mass detention, and why we're teaming up to research what actually works in the immigration system.

posts.text: ICE Fails Again to Publish Required Detention Data. For the second time this year, the agency has failed to publish the required information about immigration enforcement on schedule this week. Transparency isn't what you say, it's what you do.
posts.quote: ICE Fails Again to Publish Required Detention Data. For the second time this year, the agency has failed to publish the required information about immigration enforcement on schedule this week. Transparency isn't what you say, it's what you do.

posts.text: The Soft Suspension of Habeas Corpus. ICE is ignoring habeas corpus orders so routinely that a federal judge is threatening criminal contempt. New filings from Minnesota show what a de facto suspension of due process looks like.
posts.quote: The Soft Suspension of Habeas Corpus. ICE is ignoring habeas corpus orders so routinely that a federal judge is threatening criminal contempt. New filings from Minnesota show what a de facto suspension of due process looks like.

posts.text: Digging Into ICE's Messy 287(g) Data w/ Andrew Thrasher. I sat down with Andrew Thrasher to talk about why ICE's 287(g) data is so hard to work with, what he's built to make it usable, and why getting this right matters for accountability.
posts.quote: Digging Into ICE's Messy 287(g) Data w/ Andrew Thrasher. I sat down with Andrew Thrasher to talk about why ICE's 287(g) data is so hard to work with, what he's built to make it usable, and why getting this right matters for accountability.

posts.quote: ICE Warehouse in Hagerstown, MD. Watch now | Today I’m on the ground in Hagerstown, MD with The Real News Network, examining ICE’s detention reengineering program and what a planned warehouse detention facility looks like up close.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

posts.text: Reflections on the #AfghanEvac Global Briefing. 1,900 people from 31 countries joined today's AfghanEvac briefing. Some personal reflections on what I learned and why this issue deserves more attention.
posts.quote: Reflections on the #AfghanEvac Global Briefing. 1,900 people from 31 countries joined today's AfghanEvac briefing. Some personal reflections on what I learned and why this issue deserves more attention.

posts.text: New Polling Data Shows Immigration Enforcement Is Costing Republicans Their Own Voters. A new national survey finds 70% of Americans think the Trump administration is too focused on deportations. Immigration enforcement ranks as a top driver of defection among Trump's own 2024 voters.
posts.quote: New Polling Data Shows Immigration Enforcement Is Costing Republicans Their Own Voters. A new national survey finds 70% of Americans think the Trump administration is too focused on deportations. Immigration enforcement ranks as a top driver of defection among Trump's own 2024 voters.

immigration.quote: The gap between the rhetoric and the reality of deportation is wide and the people paying the price. For the clearest data-driven coverage of what's actually happening, follow: austinkocher.substack.com

The pressure to produce numbers has changed how the system works: by November 2025, for every one person released from ICE detention pending a hearing, 14.3 were deported directly from custody compared to a roughly one-to-two ratio the year before.

Interior deportations rose 4.6 times between the second half of 2024 and September/October 2025. But even at that accelerated pace, the administration is on track for roughly 300,000 removals unprecedented, but far short of the million-per-year target they've been claiming.

At-large arrests agents sweeping communities rather than executing targeted warrants increased by 600%. This is how you end up detaining a 19-year-old University of Utah student on her way to work and a U.S. citizen walking to lunch.

Despite claiming to target the "worst of the worst," the data shows the opposite. ICE increased arrests of people with no criminal record by 2,450% in Trump's first year the most dramatic shift in enforcement profile in the agency's history.

The Trump administration promised to deport one million people a year. One year in, the data tells a very different story about who is actually being deported, how it's happening, and what it's costing communities that never imagined they'd be targeted.

The administration promised a stronger economy. Mass deportation delivers the opposite: higher deficits, slower growth, and higher prices. For expert analysis of the real costs of enforcement, follow: austinkocher.substack.com

The agriculture sector employs undocumented workers in more than 1/3 of its positions. Construction, food service, healthcare are similarly dependent. Removing workers doesn't make American workers richer.

Brookings estimates that net migration in 2025 was likely negative for the first time in at least half a century, which is already dampening labor force growth and consumer spending. Monthly job growth could turn negative in 2026 as a result.

The Penn Wharton Budget Model estimates that a sustained mass deportation policy would cost an additional $900 billion over 10 years, even after the $170 billion already allocated in the One Big Beautiful Bill. Primary deficits would increase by $270 billion and GDP would shrink.

Undocumented immigrants pay nearly $580 billion a year in taxes and hold $1.6 trillion in annual spending power. Mass deportation removes that from the economy while adding enormous enforcement costs at least $88 billion per year just to deport one million people annually.

The Trump administration is billing mass deportation as a public safety measure. But economists, budget analysts, and researchers across the political spectrum have run the numbers and the costs are staggering. Here's what the data shows.

An agency that violates court orders and detains U.S. citizens is not enforcing the law, it's flouting it. For clear, documented analysis of what the law actually says and how ICE is violating it, read: austinkocher.substack.com

Early 2026, a fed judge in Minnesota identified 96 separate court orders ICE had violated across 74 cases since January 1st alone. At a Denver hearing, attorneys admitted that ICE arrest warrants are "riddled with mistakes" & agents couldn't explain what the court orders even say

In Minnesota, the ACLU sued after a 20-year-old U.S. citizen named Mubashir Hussen was stopped by masked ICE agents while walking to lunch. He repeatedly told them he was a citizen. They shackled him, took his fingerprints, and only released him after he showed his passport.

ICE kept making those arrests anyway. By February 2026, the ACLU filed a 98-page amended complaint alleging agents were still targeting people based on the color of their skin. Attorneys say ICE "has continued to thumb their nose both at the law and court orders."

In November 2025, a federal judge in Colorado ordered ICE to stop making warrantless arrests unless agents first establish probable cause that a person is both present illegally and a flight risk. The ruling called such arrests "unlawful."

ICE is not just aggressive in case after case, courts are finding that the agency is operating outside the law. Federal judges across the country are issuing injunctions, and ICE is violating those orders too. Here's the pattern.

A system without oversight is a system without accountability. For rigorous, data-driven analysis of immigration detention, including facility-by-facility numbers follow me at austinkocher.substack.com

Oversight has collapsed alongside the expansion. Internal watchdogs have been cut, congressional inspection rights restricted, and ICE's detainee locator system has become so unreliable that people are effectively "disappearing" for days after arrest.

The gap between the rhetoric and the reality of deportation is wide and the people paying the price. For the clearest data-driven coverage of what's actually happening, follow: austinkocher.substack.com

The pressure to produce numbers has changed how the system works: by November 2025, for every one person released from ICE detention pending a hearing, 14.3 were deported directly from custody compared to a roughly one-to-two ratio the year before.

Interior deportations rose 4.6 times between the second half of 2024 and September/October 2025. But even at that accelerated pace, the administration is on track for roughly 300,000 removals unprecedented, but far short of the million-per-year target they've been claiming.

At-large arrests agents sweeping communities rather than executing targeted warrants increased by 600%. This is how you end up detaining a 19-year-old University of Utah student on her way to work and a U.S. citizen walking to lunch.

Despite claiming to target the "worst of the worst," the data shows the opposite. ICE increased arrests of people with no criminal record by 2,450% in Trump's first year the most dramatic shift in enforcement profile in the agency's history.

The Trump administration promised to deport one million people a year. One year in, the data tells a very different story about who is actually being deported, how it's happening, and what it's costing communities that never imagined they'd be targeted.

Brookings estimates that net migration in 2025 was likely negative for the first time in at least half a century, which is already dampening labor force growth and consumer spending. Monthly job growth could turn negative in 2026 as a result.

The Penn Wharton Budget Model estimates that a sustained mass deportation policy would cost an additional $900 billion over 10 years, even after the $170 billion already allocated in the One Big Beautiful Bill. Primary deficits would increase by $270 billion and GDP would shrink.

Undocumented immigrants pay nearly $580 billion a year in taxes and hold $1.6 trillion in annual spending power. Mass deportation removes that from the economy while adding enormous enforcement costs at least $88 billion per year just to deport one million people annually.

In Minnesota, the ACLU sued after a 20-year-old U.S. citizen named Mubashir Hussen was stopped by masked ICE agents while walking to lunch. He repeatedly told them he was a citizen. They shackled him, took his fingerprints, and only released him after he showed his passport.

ICE kept making those arrests anyway. By February 2026, the ACLU filed a 98-page amended complaint alleging agents were still targeting people based on the color of their skin. Attorneys say ICE "has continued to thumb their nose both at the law and court orders."

In November 2025, a federal judge in Colorado ordered ICE to stop making warrantless arrests unless agents first establish probable cause that a person is both present illegally and a flight risk. The ruling called such arrests "unlawful."

ICE is not just aggressive in case after case, courts are finding that the agency is operating outside the law. Federal judges across the country are issuing injunctions, and ICE is violating those orders too. Here's the pattern.

A Closer Look at ICE's Enormous People Warehouse Near Hagerstown, Maryland. Third ICE Custody Death This Week, 11th Since January 1. Pejman Karshenas Najafabadi, a 59-year-old Iranian national, was pronounced dead on Sunday, March 1, 2026 at Merit Health Hospital in Natchez, Mississippi, becoming the eleventh person to die in ICE custody since the start of the 2026 calendar

Haitian Asylum Seeker Dies of Toothache in What ICE Calls "The Best Healthcare of Their Lives". Detention deaths are a foreseeable outcome of specific policy choices made by people in position of power. This post steps back to think through the broader trends and steps toward accountability.

The Human Cost of Mass Detention: ICE Reports 9th Custodial Death of 2026, 38th of Trump II Administration. ICE press release for the death of Alberto Gutierrez-Reyes debuts a strikingly callous (and ungrammatical) new claim. Full table of all 38 deaths under Trump included in post.

The Alternatives to Mass Immigrant Detention No One is Talking about w/ Claire Trickler-McNulty. Claire Trickler-McNulty and I discuss the non-detained docket, alternatives to mass detention, and why we're teaming up to research what actually works in the immigration system.

ICE Fails Again to Publish Required Detention Data. For the second time this year, the agency has failed to publish the required information about immigration enforcement on schedule this week. Transparency isn't what you say, it's what you do.

The Soft Suspension of Habeas Corpus. ICE is ignoring habeas corpus orders so routinely that a federal judge is threatening criminal contempt. New filings from Minnesota show what a de facto suspension of due process looks like.

Digging Into ICE's Messy 287(g) Data w/ Andrew Thrasher. I sat down with Andrew Thrasher to talk about why ICE's 287(g) data is so hard to work with, what he's built to make it usable, and why getting this right matters for accountability.

ICE Warehouse in Hagerstown, MD. Watch now | Today I’m on the ground in Hagerstown, MD with The Real News Network, examining ICE’s detention reengineering program and what a planned warehouse detention facility looks like up close.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

New Polling Data Shows Immigration Enforcement Is Costing Republicans Their Own Voters. A new national survey finds 70% of Americans think the Trump administration is too focused on deportations. Immigration enforcement ranks as a top driver of defection among Trump's own 2024 voters.
